subject: Property Law
lesson_title: Mortgages: Creation, the Equity of Redemption, Clogs, Possession, and the Power of Sale

questions.question: Under section 89 of the Land and Conveyancing Law Reform Act 2009, what form must all new mortgages of freehold or leasehold land created on or after 1 December 2009 take?

options.A: A mortgage by demise or sub-demise
options.B: A charge by deed expressed to be by way of legal mortgage
options.C: A registered mortgage by way of assignment
options.D: An equitable mortgage by deposit of title deeds

questions.explanation: Section 89 LCLRA 2009 abolished the old forms of mortgage — by demise and sub-demise — for new mortgages created from 1 December 2009. All new mortgages must take the form of a charge by deed. The borrower retains legal ownership of the property throughout.

questions.question: What is the key consequence for a borrower of the change introduced by section 89 of the Land and Conveyancing Law Reform Act 2009?

options.A: The borrower loses legal title to the property on execution of the mortgage
options.B: The borrower must register a caution against their own title
options.C: The borrower retains legal ownership of the property throughout the mortgage
options.D: The borrower becomes a leaseholder of the property during the mortgage term

questions.explanation: Under section 89 LCLRA 2009, the new mortgage by charge does not transfer ownership to the lender. The borrower retains legal ownership of the property throughout. This is a fundamental change from the old mortgage by demise, under which legal title passed to the mortgagee.

questions.question: What is the equity of redemption in mortgage law?

options.A: The lender's right to sell the property when the borrower defaults
options.B: A statutory right allowing the borrower to redeem the mortgage at any time before sale
options.C: The borrower's equitable right to redeem the mortgage on paying off the debt, and the totality of their interest in the mortgaged property
options.D: A court order preventing the lender from selling the mortgaged property

questions.explanation: The equity of redemption is the borrower's equitable right to redeem the mortgage by paying off the debt. Equity developed this right to protect borrowers from unconscionable terms. It represents the totality of the borrower's interest in the mortgaged property and courts will not permit it to be clogs or fettered.

questions.question: In Browne v Ryan [1901], the Irish court held that an option to purchase given to the mortgagee as part of the mortgage transaction was void as a clog on the equity of redemption. What is a clog on the equity of redemption?

options.A: Any term that increases the interest rate payable on the mortgage
options.B: Any term that prevents or penalises the mortgagor from redeeming the mortgage when entitled to do so
options.C: A restriction on the mortgagor's right to let the property during the mortgage term
options.D: A requirement that the mortgagor obtain the mortgagee's consent before disposing of the property

questions.explanation: A clog on the equity of redemption is any term that prevents or penalises the mortgagor from freely redeeming when entitled to do so. In Browne v Ryan [1901], the option to purchase granted to the mortgagee was held to be such a clog because it could prevent the mortgagor from recovering their property on repayment.

questions.question: In Kreglinger v New Patagonia Meat and Cold Storage Co [1914], the House of Lords held that a collateral advantage reserved by the mortgagee was valid and did not constitute a clog on the equity of redemption. What was the key distinction?

options.A: The collateral advantage was part of an entirely independent and contemporaneous commercial agreement
options.B: The collateral advantage was agreed after the mortgage was created
options.C: The mortgagor had received independent legal advice before agreeing to the collateral advantage
options.D: The collateral advantage expired automatically on redemption of the mortgage

questions.explanation: In Kreglinger [1914], the House of Lords held that a collateral advantage is valid and does not constitute a clog where it forms part of an independent commercial agreement and is not a term of the mortgage itself. The right of first refusal on sheepskins was an independent collateral advantage forming part of a wider commercial arrangement.

questions.question: In Start Mortgages v Gunn [2011], Dunne J identified a lacuna in the Land and Conveyancing Law Reform Act 2009 in relation to mortgagee possession proceedings. What was the lacuna?

options.A: Section 97 required a court order before a lender could take possession of a dwelling but the LCLRA 2009 contained no procedure for post-2009 charges to apply for such an order
options.B: Section 97 abolished the lender's right to possession entirely for residential dwellings
options.C: Section 97 required the borrower's consent before any possession proceedings could be issued
options.D: Section 97 required mediation before court proceedings for possession could be brought

questions.explanation: In Start Mortgages v Gunn [2011], Dunne J held that while section 97 LCLRA 2009 required a court order before a chargee could take possession of a dwelling, the LCLRA 2009 provided no procedure by which a post-2009 chargee could apply for such an order. This lacuna left lenders under post-2009 charges without a mechanism to seek possession.

questions.question: How was the lacuna identified in Start Mortgages v Gunn [2011] resolved?

options.A: The Supreme Court read the LCLRA 2009 as incorporating the old procedure by analogy
options.B: Section 1 of the Land and Conveyancing Law Reform Act 2013 retrospectively applied the court order requirement to all mortgages regardless of date of creation
options.C: The Land Registry introduced a new administrative procedure for possession applications
options.D: The Oireachtas passed an emergency regulation suspending the court order requirement

questions.explanation: Section 1 of the Land and Conveyancing Law Reform Act 2013 fixed the lacuna retrospectively. It applied the court order requirement to all mortgages — whether created before or after 1 December 2009 — meaning all lenders seeking possession of a dwelling must obtain a court order.

questions.question: Under section 100 of the Land and Conveyancing Law Reform Act 2009, when does a chargee's power of sale arise?

options.A: When the borrower is twelve months in arrears of repayments
options.B: When the mortgage money has become due
options.C: Only after the chargee obtains a court order
options.D: At any time after the mortgage is executed, regardless of default

questions.explanation: Under section 100 LCLRA 2009, the chargee's power of sale arises when the mortgage money has become due — that is, when the borrower is in default. The power arises automatically at that point, though it must be exercised in accordance with the statutory duty of care.

questions.question: Under section 103 of the Land and Conveyancing Law Reform Act 2009, what is the duty imposed on a mortgagee exercising the power of sale?

options.A: To maximise the sale price through competitive public auction only
options.B: To consult the borrower before accepting any offer
options.C: To obtain the best price reasonably obtainable at the time of sale
options.D: To wait for market conditions to improve before selling

questions.explanation: Section 103 LCLRA 2009 imposes a duty on the mortgagee to take reasonable care to obtain the best price reasonably obtainable at the time of sale. This is a statutory codification of the equitable duty confirmed in Cuckmere Brick Co v Mutual Finance [1971].

questions.question: What principle was established in Cuckmere Brick Co v Mutual Finance [1971] in relation to the mortgagee's duty when exercising the power of sale?

options.A: A mortgagee has no duty of care to the mortgagor when selling the property
options.B: A mortgagee must obtain the highest possible price irrespective of prevailing market conditions
options.C: A mortgagee owes a duty of care to obtain the true market value of the mortgaged property
options.D: A mortgagee can sell at any price once it has advertised the property

questions.explanation: In Cuckmere Brick Co v Mutual Finance [1971], the English Court of Appeal held that a mortgagee owes the mortgagor a duty to take reasonable care to obtain the true market value of the property when exercising the power of sale. This duty of care has been adopted in Irish law and is now codified in section 103 LCLRA 2009.

questions.question: In Zurich Bank v McConnon [2011], the Irish High Court held that where a mortgagee sold mortgaged property at an undervalue in breach of the section 103 duty, the consequence was:

options.A: The sale was void and could be set aside at the mortgagor's election
options.B: The mortgagor was entitled to damages representing the difference between the price obtained and the true market value
options.C: The mortgage was deemed satisfied by the proceeds of the undervalue sale
options.D: The purchaser acquired only an equitable interest in the property

questions.explanation: In Zurich Bank v McConnon [2011], the Irish High Court confirmed that breach of the section 103 duty by selling at an undervalue sounds in damages. The mortgagor is entitled to recover the difference between the price obtained and the best price reasonably obtainable. The sale itself is not set aside.

questions.question: In Stepstone Mortgage Funding Ltd v Fitzell [2012], the Irish court went further than the damages remedy. What additional remedy was recognised where a mortgagee threatened to sell at a gross undervalue?

options.A: An order for specific performance requiring the mortgagee to renegotiate the loan terms
options.B: A declaration that the mortgage was void for breach of the duty of care
options.C: An order of possession in favour of the mortgagor
options.D: An injunction to prevent the sale at a gross undervalue

questions.explanation: In Stepstone v Fitzell [2012], the court recognised that an injunction could be granted to prevent a sale at a gross undervalue. This extends the protection beyond mere damages — where the breach is imminent and egregious, the mortgagor may seek to restrain the sale before it takes place.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of the Code of Conduct on Mortgage Arrears (CCMA) in the context of residential mortgage enforcement?

options.A: To abolish the lender's right to seek possession of residential property
options.B: To set out the procedures lenders must follow before commencing legal proceedings against borrowers in mortgage arrears
options.C: To fix interest rates for borrowers who fall into arrears on residential mortgages
options.D: To provide a government guarantee for residential mortgage debt

questions.explanation: The CCMA sets out the procedures that regulated lenders must follow before commencing legal proceedings against borrowers in arrears on their principal private residence. It requires lenders to engage with borrowers, explore alternatives to possession, and adhere to a structured arrears process before resorting to enforcement.

questions.question: The Personal Insolvency Acts introduced a framework to assist over-indebted borrowers in Ireland. Which of the following is a key feature of that framework?

options.A: Automatic discharge of all mortgage debt after five years of non-payment
options.B: A Debt Relief Notice allowing borrowers with low income and assets to write off unsecured debt up to a threshold
options.C: An absolute prohibition on possession proceedings while a personal insolvency arrangement is in place
options.D: Automatic conversion of residential mortgages to interest-free loans

questions.explanation: The Personal Insolvency Acts introduced three mechanisms: the Debt Relief Notice (unsecured debt write-off for low-income borrowers); the Debt Settlement Arrangement (unsecured debt); and the Personal Insolvency Arrangement (secured and unsecured debt). These provide a structured statutory alternative to bankruptcy for over-indebted borrowers.

questions.question: Prior to 1 December 2009, what were the two main forms of mortgage used in Ireland?

options.A: Legal mortgage and equitable mortgage only
options.B: Mortgage by demise (or sub-demise) and mortgage by charge
options.C: Registered charge and unregistered conveyance of title
options.D: Puisne mortgage and floating charge

questions.explanation: Prior to the LCLRA 2009, the two principal forms of mortgage in Ireland were the mortgage by demise (or sub-demise) — where the borrower granted a long lease to the lender — and the mortgage by charge. Section 89 LCLRA 2009 abolished the demise forms for all new mortgages from 1 December 2009.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about the old-style mortgage by demise (pre-LCLRA 2009) is correct?

options.A: The borrower retained legal ownership of the mortgaged property throughout
options.B: The lender obtained a right of sale only after obtaining a court order
options.C: Legal title to the property passed to the mortgagee for the duration of the mortgage term
options.D: The borrower received an equitable interest in the property during the mortgage term

questions.explanation: Under the old mortgage by demise, the borrower granted the lender a very long lease (typically 3,000 years), which meant that legal title passed to the mortgagee for the duration of the term. This is why the LCLRA 2009 reform — which retains legal ownership with the borrower — is such a significant change.

questions.question: Which of the following formulations best captures the doctrine that once a mortgage, always a mortgage?

options.A: A mortgage cannot be varied by agreement between the parties after it is created
options.B: Equity will not permit the mortgagee to use the mortgage transaction to acquire any advantage beyond repayment of the loan with interest
options.C: A mortgage must be repaid in full before the mortgagor can redeem
options.D: A mortgagee who acquires legal title to the mortgaged property cannot resell it to a third party

questions.explanation: The doctrine that once a mortgage, always a mortgage reflects equity's insistence that the mortgage transaction is a security for repayment — nothing more. Equity will not allow the mortgagee to use it to obtain advantages or interests beyond repayment of the debt with interest. This principle underpins the clog on equity of redemption doctrine.

questions.question: In Browne v Ryan [1901], the option to purchase granted to the mortgagee was held void as a clog on the equity of redemption. What test was applied to determine whether a term amounted to a clog?

options.A: Whether the term gave the mortgagee a beneficial interest in the mortgaged property
options.B: Whether the term was unreasonable or oppressive in the circumstances
options.C: Whether the term prevented the mortgagor from getting back the mortgaged property on repayment of the debt free from any interest of the mortgagee
options.D: Whether the term had been explained to the mortgagor before the mortgage was executed

questions.explanation: The test for a clog on the equity of redemption asks whether the term prevents the mortgagor from recovering the mortgaged property free and clear on repayment of the debt. In Browne v Ryan, the option to purchase allowed the mortgagee to acquire the property even after repayment — this was exactly such a clog.

questions.question: The principle in Kreglinger v New Patagonia Meat and Cold Storage Co [1914] that a valid independent collateral advantage does not constitute a clog on the equity of redemption requires what?

options.A: The advantage must have been agreed after the mortgage was created
options.B: The advantage must be of a purely financial nature and proportionate to the loan
options.C: The advantage must be part of a genuine independent commercial transaction and not merely a disguised part of the mortgage terms
options.D: The advantage must cease automatically on redemption of the mortgage

questions.explanation: Kreglinger [1914] HL established that a collateral advantage is valid where it is genuinely independent — part of a separate commercial arrangement rather than a dressed-up term of the mortgage. If the advantage is in substance part of the mortgage, it is a clog. If it is truly independent, it can survive redemption.

questions.question: Under section 97 of the Land and Conveyancing Law Reform Act 2009, a chargee seeking possession of a dwelling must:

options.A: Give six months notice to the borrower before issuing proceedings
options.B: Apply to the Property Registration Authority for an order of possession
options.C: Obtain a court order before taking possession
options.D: Demonstrate that the borrower has been in arrears for at least twelve consecutive months

questions.explanation: Section 97 LCLRA 2009 requires that a chargee must obtain a court order before taking possession of a dwelling. This was confirmed and clarified by the LCLRA 2013 which applied the requirement to all mortgages regardless of whether they were created before or after 1 December 2009.

questions.question: Section 1 of the Land and Conveyancing Law Reform Act 2013 resolved the lacuna identified in Start Mortgages v Gunn [2011] by:

options.A: Abolishing the court order requirement for post-2009 charges
options.B: Applying the court order requirement retrospectively to all mortgages regardless of date of creation
options.C: Introducing a new administrative process for possession applications outside the courts
options.D: Granting lenders a right to take possession by self-help where the borrower had abandoned the property

questions.explanation: The LCLRA 2013, section 1 fixed the lacuna by applying the court order requirement to all mortgages — whether created before or after 1 December 2009. This ensured all lenders must go to court to obtain possession of a dwelling and ended the procedural uncertainty identified in Start Mortgages v Gunn.

questions.question: Under section 100 of the Land and Conveyancing Law Reform Act 2009, the chargee's statutory power of sale arises when:

options.A: The borrower has missed three consecutive mortgage repayments
options.B: The mortgage money has become due
options.C: The borrower has failed to remedy a breach of covenant within a specified notice period
options.D: The chargee has obtained a court order for possession

questions.explanation: Section 100 LCLRA 2009 provides that the statutory power of sale arises when the mortgage money has become due. This is the trigger point — once the borrower is in default and the sum is due, the power of sale arises, though it must be exercised in compliance with the section 103 duty.

questions.question: What is the standard of care imposed on a mortgagee under section 103 of the Land and Conveyancing Law Reform Act 2009 when exercising the power of sale?

options.A: To achieve the highest possible price without any qualification
options.B: To advertise the property widely and accept the highest offer received
options.C: To take reasonable care to obtain the best price reasonably obtainable at the time of sale
options.D: To obtain a current market valuation and sell at no less than that figure

questions.explanation: Section 103 LCLRA 2009 codifies the duty established in Cuckmere Brick Co v Mutual Finance [1971]: the mortgagee must take reasonable care to obtain the best price reasonably obtainable at the time of sale. The standard is one of reasonable care — not an absolute guarantee of the highest possible price.

questions.question: Which English Court of Appeal case established that a mortgagee owes the mortgagor a duty of care to obtain the true market value when exercising the power of sale?

options.A: Browne v Ryan [1901]
options.B: Kreglinger v New Patagonia Meat and Cold Storage Co [1914]
options.C: Start Mortgages v Gunn [2011]
options.D: Cuckmere Brick Co v Mutual Finance [1971]

questions.explanation: Cuckmere Brick Co v Mutual Finance [1971] CA established the duty of care owed by a mortgagee when exercising the power of sale — to take reasonable care to obtain the true market value. This duty has been adopted in Irish law and is now codified in section 103 LCLRA 2009.

questions.question: In Zurich Bank v McConnon [2011], what remedy was available to a mortgagor where the mortgagee sold the mortgaged property below its true market value in breach of the section 103 duty?

options.A: The mortgagor could have the sale set aside and the property reconveyed
options.B: Damages equivalent to the shortfall between the price obtained and the best price reasonably obtainable
options.C: A right of pre-emption entitling the mortgagor to repurchase the property
options.D: An automatic reduction of the mortgage debt to nil

questions.explanation: In Zurich Bank v McConnon [2011], the High Court confirmed that breach of the section 103 duty sounds in damages. The mortgagor can recover the difference between the price obtained and the best price reasonably obtainable. The sale is not set aside — the remedy is compensatory.

questions.question: In Stepstone Mortgage Funding Ltd v Fitzell [2012], what remedy was available to prevent a mortgagee from proceeding with a sale at a gross undervalue?

options.A: A right of redemption allowing the mortgagor to repay and prevent the sale
options.B: An injunction to restrain the threatened sale at a gross undervalue
options.C: A court order compelling the mortgagee to accept a higher offer
options.D: A statutory charge in favour of the mortgagor protecting their equity of redemption

questions.explanation: In Stepstone v Fitzell [2012], the court recognised that an injunction could be granted to prevent a mortgagee from proceeding with a sale at a gross undervalue before it takes place. This extends the remedy beyond damages to allow pre-emptive protection of the mortgagor's equity of redemption.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the purpose of the Code of Conduct on Mortgage Arrears in the residential mortgage context?

options.A: It sets a statutory cap on the interest rates chargeable on residential mortgages in arrears
options.B: It provides a regulatory framework requiring lenders to engage with borrowers in arrears and consider alternatives before enforcement
options.C: It converts arrears on residential mortgages into unsecured debt automatically after twelve months
options.D: It requires lenders to write off residential mortgage arrears after a fixed period of non-engagement

questions.explanation: The Code of Conduct on Mortgage Arrears (CCMA) requires regulated lenders to follow a structured engagement process with borrowers in arrears on their principal private residence — including considering alternatives to possession — before commencing legal enforcement proceedings. It is a regulatory requirement, not a complete bar to enforcement.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes the Personal Insolvency Arrangement (PIA) introduced under the Personal Insolvency Acts?

options.A: A mechanism for writing off all unsecured debt for low-income borrowers
options.B: A mechanism dealing with unsecured debt only, excluding mortgage debt
options.C: An automatic discharge of secured debt after three years of non-payment
options.D: A statutory arrangement dealing with both secured and unsecured debt, allowing restructuring of residential mortgage debt

questions.explanation: The Personal Insolvency Arrangement (PIA) is one of three mechanisms introduced by the Personal Insolvency Acts. Unlike the Debt Relief Notice (unsecured debt only) and Debt Settlement Arrangement (unsecured debt), the PIA deals with both secured and unsecured debt and allows for restructuring of residential mortgage obligations.

questions.question: Which year did section 89 of the Land and Conveyancing Law Reform Act 2009 come into force, abolishing the old forms of mortgage?

options.A: 1 January 2010
options.B: 1 December 2009
options.C: 1 December 2010
options.D: 1 January 2009

questions.explanation: Section 89 LCLRA 2009 came into force on 1 December 2009. All mortgages created on or after that date must be by way of charge. The old forms of mortgage — by demise or sub-demise — were abolished for new transactions from that date.

questions.question: Under Irish mortgage law, which of the following terms in a mortgage deed would most clearly constitute a clog on the equity of redemption?

options.A: A term allowing the mortgagee to inspect the property annually
options.B: A term requiring the mortgagor to insure the property
options.C: A term granting the mortgagee an option to purchase the property at any time
options.D: A term setting a fixed interest rate for the first five years of the mortgage

questions.explanation: An option to purchase granted to the mortgagee is the classic example of a clog on the equity of redemption — as established in Browne v Ryan [1901]. Such a term would allow the mortgagee to acquire the property even after repayment, preventing the mortgagor from recovering their property free and clear. Inspection clauses and insurance requirements are standard mortgage terms, not clogs.

questions.question: Which court decided Start Mortgages v Gunn [2011], which identified the lacuna in the LCLRA 2009 regarding possession proceedings?

options.A: The Supreme Court of Ireland
options.B: The Court of Appeal
options.C: The High Court (Dunne J)
options.D: The Circuit Court

questions.explanation: Start Mortgages v Gunn [2011] was decided by the High Court, with judgment delivered by Dunne J. Dunne J identified that section 97 LCLRA 2009 required a court order for possession of a dwelling but the Act had omitted to provide a procedure by which post-2009 chargees could apply for such an order.

questions.question: The Land and Conveyancing Law Reform Act 2013 applied the court order requirement under section 97 of the LCLRA 2009 to:

options.A: Post-2009 charges only, confirming the position established in Start Mortgages v Gunn
options.B: Pre-2009 mortgages by demise only
options.C: Commercial mortgages and charges only
options.D: All mortgages and charges regardless of the date they were created

questions.explanation: The LCLRA 2013 applied the court order requirement to all mortgages and charges, regardless of whether they were created before or after 1 December 2009. This removed the procedural distinction and uncertainty highlighted in Start Mortgages v Gunn [2011].

questions.question: Which of the following correctly states the relationship between the mortgage and the equity of redemption?

options.A: The equity of redemption is extinguished once the mortgage is registered against the title
options.B: The equity of redemption is the borrower's right in equity to reclaim the property on paying off the debt, which courts will not permit to be clogs or fettered by the mortgagee
options.C: The equity of redemption can be waived by the borrower in the mortgage deed
options.D: The equity of redemption only arises after the legal date for redemption has passed

questions.explanation: The equity of redemption is the mortgagor's equitable right to redeem on repaying the debt. Courts of equity developed this right to protect borrowers and will not permit mortgagees to include terms that clog, fetter, or impair it. It cannot be waived by the mortgagor in the mortgage deed — once a mortgage, always a mortgage.

questions.question: Section 89 of the Land and Conveyancing Law Reform Act 2009 abolished which forms of mortgage for transactions from 1 December 2009?

options.A: Equitable mortgages and puisne mortgages
options.B: Mortgages by demise and mortgages by sub-demise
options.C: Registered charges and unregistered mortgages
options.D: Interest-only mortgages and endowment mortgages

questions.explanation: Section 89 LCLRA 2009 abolished the mortgage by demise (where a freehold borrower granted a long lease to the lender) and the mortgage by sub-demise (where a leasehold borrower sub-let to the lender) for all new mortgages created from 1 December 2009. All new mortgages must be by way of charge.

questions.question: The section 103 duty to obtain the best price reasonably obtainable applies to which party when exercising the power of sale?

options.A: The mortgagor, when attempting to sell the property to redeem the mortgage
options.B: The estate agent appointed by the mortgagee to sell the property
options.C: The mortgagee, when exercising the statutory power of sale under section 100
options.D: The court, when ordering a sale on the application of either party

questions.explanation: The section 103 duty is imposed on the mortgagee — the lender — when exercising the statutory power of sale under section 100. It requires the mortgagee to take reasonable care to obtain the best price reasonably obtainable at the time. The duty protects the mortgagor's equity of redemption and any subsequent encumbrancers.

questions.question: Kreglinger v New Patagonia Meat and Cold Storage Co [1914] was decided by:

options.A: The Irish Supreme Court
options.B: The English Court of Appeal
options.C: The House of Lords
options.D: The Privy Council

questions.explanation: Kreglinger v New Patagonia Meat and Cold Storage Co [1914] was decided by the House of Lords. It is the leading authority on the validity of independent collateral advantages in mortgage transactions, distinguishing between terms that are genuinely independent of the mortgage and those that constitute clogs on the equity of redemption.

questions.question: Which of the following accurately describes the position of a mortgagor under a post-2009 charge by deed under section 89 LCLRA 2009?

options.A: The mortgagor loses legal title on execution but retains an equity of redemption
options.B: The mortgagor retains full legal ownership of the property throughout the mortgage term
options.C: The mortgagor becomes a tenant of the mortgagee for the duration of the mortgage term
options.D: The mortgagor holds an equitable freehold only until the charge is discharged

questions.explanation: Under a post-2009 charge by deed, the mortgagor retains full legal ownership of the property throughout. The charge grants the lender a security interest over the property but does not transfer legal title. This is the fundamental difference from the old mortgage by demise.

questions.question: Browne v Ryan [1901] is the leading Irish authority on which aspect of mortgage law?

options.A: The mortgagee's duty of care when exercising the power of sale
options.B: The clog on the equity of redemption doctrine — specifically that an option to purchase granted to the mortgagee is void
options.C: The statutory requirement for a court order before possession of a dwelling
options.D: The validity of independent collateral advantages in mortgage transactions

questions.explanation: Browne v Ryan [1901] is the leading Irish authority on the clog on the equity of redemption doctrine. The court held that an option to purchase granted to the mortgagee as part of the mortgage transaction was void as a clog, because it could prevent the mortgagor from recovering the property free and clear on repayment.

questions.question: Where a mortgagee exercises the power of sale without complying with the section 103 duty to obtain the best price reasonably obtainable, what is the effect on the purchaser's title?

options.A: The purchaser acquires no title and the sale is void
options.B: The purchaser acquires a voidable title which the mortgagor can set aside
options.C: The purchaser acquires good title but the mortgagee is liable in damages to the mortgagor
options.D: The purchaser takes subject to the mortgagor's equity of redemption

questions.explanation: Where a mortgagee sells in breach of the section 103 duty, the purchaser still acquires good title — the sale is not set aside. The mortgagor's remedy is in damages against the mortgagee, as confirmed in Zurich Bank v McConnon [2011]. This protects the integrity of property transactions while compensating the mortgagor.

questions.question: Which of the following is the correct description of the Personal Insolvency Acts framework for over-indebted borrowers in Ireland?

options.A: A single procedure applying to all debts of whatever nature
options.B: Three statutory mechanisms: the Debt Relief Notice, the Debt Settlement Arrangement, and the Personal Insolvency Arrangement
options.C: A court-supervised scheme applicable only to corporate borrowers
options.D: A voluntary scheme administered by the banks rather than the courts

questions.explanation: The Personal Insolvency Acts introduced three mechanisms for over-indebted individuals: the Debt Relief Notice (low-income borrowers, unsecured debt up to a threshold); the Debt Settlement Arrangement (unsecured debt); and the Personal Insolvency Arrangement (secured and unsecured debt, including residential mortgage restructuring).

questions.question: Under which section of the Land and Conveyancing Law Reform Act 2009 does the statutory power of sale for chargees arise?

options.A: Section 97
options.B: Section 100
options.C: Section 103
options.D: Section 89

questions.explanation: The statutory power of sale for chargees arises under section 100 LCLRA 2009 when the mortgage money has become due. Section 97 deals with possession, section 103 imposes the duty to obtain the best price, and section 89 governs the form of new mortgages.

questions.question: Under which section of the Land and Conveyancing Law Reform Act 2009 is the duty to obtain the best price reasonably obtainable on a sale imposed on a mortgagee?

options.A: Section 89
options.B: Section 97
options.C: Section 100
options.D: Section 103

questions.explanation: Section 103 LCLRA 2009 imposes the duty to take reasonable care to obtain the best price reasonably obtainable on a sale. This statutory codification reflects the equitable duty established in Cuckmere Brick Co v Mutual Finance [1971].

questions.question: Which of the following statements about the mortgage by charge under section 89 LCLRA 2009 is correct?

options.A: The charge operates as a transfer of legal title to the lender as security for the loan
options.B: The charge grants the lender a security interest over the property without transferring legal ownership to the lender
options.C: The charge automatically converts to a mortgage by demise if the borrower defaults
options.D: The charge requires registration in both the Land Registry and the Companies Registration Office

questions.explanation: Under section 89 LCLRA 2009, a charge by deed grants the lender a security interest over the property as security for the loan. Crucially, legal ownership remains with the borrower throughout. The charge does not transfer title — it is a pure security interest.

questions.question: In the context of mortgage enforcement, what did Dunne J hold in Start Mortgages v Gunn [2011] regarding lenders relying on pre-2009 procedures after the LCLRA 2009 came into force?

options.A: Pre-2009 lenders could continue to use the old summary summons procedure for possession
options.B: All lenders must use the new statutory procedure under section 97 regardless of when the mortgage was created
options.C: Section 97 LCLRA 2009 required a court order but provided no procedure for post-2009 chargees to obtain one, creating a lacuna
options.D: Post-2009 chargees could take possession by self-help without a court order pending new legislation

questions.explanation: In Start Mortgages v Gunn [2011], Dunne J identified that section 97 LCLRA 2009 required a court order before a chargee could take possession of a dwelling but the 2009 Act failed to provide a procedure for post-2009 chargees to apply for such an order. This lacuna was subsequently remedied by the LCLRA 2013.

questions.question: Which of the following mortgage terms would be most likely to constitute a clog on the equity of redemption under Irish law?

options.A: A requirement to maintain the property in good repair
options.B: A requirement to obtain the lender's consent before letting the property
options.C: A term providing that the mortgagee could acquire the mortgaged property at a fixed price within five years of the mortgage
options.D: A clause setting out the lender's right to appoint a receiver on default

questions.explanation: A term allowing the mortgagee to acquire the mortgaged property at a fixed price is the paradigm clog on the equity of redemption — it prevents the mortgagor from redeeming the property free and clear of any interest of the mortgagee. Repair clauses, consent to let clauses, and receiver clauses are standard enforceable mortgage terms.

questions.question: The Code of Conduct on Mortgage Arrears applies to which category of mortgage?

options.A: All mortgages including commercial and buy-to-let mortgages
options.B: Mortgages secured on the borrower's principal private residence only
options.C: Mortgages created after 1 December 2009 only
options.D: All residential mortgages regardless of whether the property is owner-occupied

questions.explanation: The Code of Conduct on Mortgage Arrears applies to mortgages secured on the borrower's principal private residence. It requires regulated lenders to follow a structured engagement process before commencing enforcement. It does not apply to buy-to-let mortgages or commercial mortgages.

questions.question: What key change did the Land and Conveyancing Law Reform Act 2013 make to the court order requirement under section 97 LCLRA 2009?

options.A: It raised the threshold for obtaining a court order to twelve months of arrears
options.B: It abolished the court order requirement for mortgages created before 2009
options.C: It confirmed that the court order requirement applies to all mortgages regardless of when they were created
options.D: It introduced a fast-track court procedure for lenders with post-2009 charges

questions.explanation: The LCLRA 2013 confirmed that the court order requirement under section 97 applies to all mortgages — whether created before or after 1 December 2009. This retrospective application resolved the lacuna identified in Start Mortgages v Gunn and ensured all lenders seeking possession of a dwelling must go to court.

questions.question: In mortgage law, which of the following reflects the significance of Kreglinger v New Patagonia Meat and Cold Storage Co [1914] as distinct from Browne v Ryan [1901]?

options.A: Kreglinger established that all collateral advantages in mortgage transactions are void
options.B: Kreglinger distinguished between terms that are a genuine clog on redemption and genuinely independent collateral advantages that can remain enforceable
options.C: Kreglinger abolished the equity of redemption doctrine for commercial mortgages
options.D: Kreglinger held that the equity of redemption cannot be exercised after the legal redemption date has passed

questions.explanation: Browne v Ryan [1901] held an option to purchase in a mortgage void as a clog. Kreglinger [1914] HL established the important qualification: a genuinely independent collateral advantage — one that is not simply a dressed-up term of the mortgage — is valid and can remain enforceable even after redemption. The key is whether the advantage is truly independent or part of the mortgage transaction itself.

questions.question: Under what section of the Land and Conveyancing Law Reform Act 2009 is a chargee required to obtain a court order before taking possession of a dwelling?

options.A: Section 89
options.B: Section 97
options.C: Section 100
options.D: Section 103

questions.explanation: Section 97 LCLRA 2009 requires a chargee to obtain a court order before taking possession of a dwelling. As confirmed by the LCLRA 2013, this requirement applies to all mortgages regardless of when they were created. Section 89 governs the form of new mortgages; section 100 governs the power of sale; section 103 imposes the duty of care on sale.

questions.question: Which of the following best summarises the combined effect of Cuckmere Brick Co v Mutual Finance [1971] and section 103 of the Land and Conveyancing Law Reform Act 2009 on a mortgagee exercising the power of sale?

options.A: The mortgagee must achieve the highest possible price at all times regardless of market conditions
options.B: The mortgagee has no duty to the mortgagor once the power of sale has arisen
options.C: The mortgagee must take reasonable care to obtain the best price reasonably obtainable, with liability in damages for breach
options.D: The mortgagee must obtain the mortgagor's consent before completing any sale

questions.explanation: The combined effect of Cuckmere Brick Co v Mutual Finance [1971] and section 103 LCLRA 2009 is that a mortgagee must take reasonable care to obtain the best price reasonably obtainable at the time of sale. Breach sounds in damages (Zurich Bank v McConnon [2011]) and, where a gross undervalue is threatened, may be restrained by injunction (Stepstone v Fitzell [2012]).
